one_line_takeaway: By extending GCG with a syntax-prefix loss and vocabulary masking, the paper shows that unreadable, gibberish-looking prompts can be automatically optimized to force real commercial LLM agents (Mistral LeChat, ChatGLM) into precise, context-dependent tool misuse that silently exfiltrates users' conversation keywords and PII to an attacker URL, with transfer success rates around 70-100% syntax correctness on production systems.
key_finding: The optimized obfuscated prompts transfer from local open-weight models to real black-box commercial agents with high reliability. On Mistral LeChat, the PII-exfiltration prompt T10 achieved 100% syntax correctness, 70.4% PII precision, 41.2% PII recall, and 88.0% context GPT score in a real-world end-to-end test; the same T10 prompt scored 79.8% PII precision locally, so transfer to the live product incurs a modest ~9-point precision drop (the abstract's 'nearly 80%' refers to the local number). For information exfiltration on real products, prompt T1 on LeChat reached 84.7% syntax / 70.1% extraction precision, and T5 on ChatGLM reached 99.0% syntax / 62.5% precision. Locally, prompts consistently hit >=80% syntax correctness and ~80% word-extraction precision across 9B/12B/70B text models and the 3B visual model, while carrying perplexities in the thousands (vs <10 for natural text), confirming simultaneous effectiveness and obfuscation. Optimized prompts consistently beat their un-obfuscated natural-language equivalents (e.g., LeChat info-exfil syntax 84.7% for T1 vs 59.1% for manual N1; LeChat PII syntax 100% for T10 vs 44.0% for manual N6).
core_contribution: Imprompter introduces a new class of attack on LLM agents: automatically-computed, obfuscated adversarial prompts (both text and image) that force an agent to emit a precise, syntactically-correct, context-dependent tool invocation while hiding their true purpose from human inspection. The paper's own technical contribution is 'Tool Misuse GCG' (Algorithm 1), an extension of the existing Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) discrete-optimization method. The extensions over vanilla GCG are: (1) supporting multi-turn conversation context c rather than a single fixed prefix; (2) allowing the optimization target y to be context-dependent on the conversation c (e.g., extracting whatever PII/keywords a given conversation contains) rather than a fixed short string like 'Sure, here is...'; (3) adding a syntax-prefix loss term L_syn (weighted by lambda) that specifically penalizes divergence from the exact tool-call syntax prefix, combined into a joint loss L_joint = L + lambda*L_syn; and (4) achieving obfuscation not via a loss term but by masking the candidate vocabulary during top-k selection (e.g., a 'non-english' mask that forces tokens toward other-language/Unicode characters) plus re-initializing from less-obfuscated prior results with keywords replaced by delimiters. A parallel continuous-space image attack (Adam optimizer, pixel values clipped to [0,1)) is also contributed. The authors are careful to distinguish this from prior prompt injection (which uses human-readable handcrafted English) and jailbreaking (where the USER is the attacker and the objective is a simple 'Sure'-style content-policy violation, not a precise tool call).
threat_model: The attacker targets a BENIGN user and their LLM agent (explicitly contrasted with jailbreaking, where the user is malicious). The attacker's goal is to violate the confidentiality and integrity of user resources connected to the agent by forcing the agent to misuse a tool -- demonstrated concretely as exfiltrating conversation keywords and PII by making the agent invoke a URL/web-access or markdown-image-rendering tool that fetches an attacker-controlled URL (velocity.show) with the stolen data encoded in the path/query. The attacker is assumed to have WHITE-BOX gradient access to an open-weight LLM in the same family as the target production model (justified because many products start from open weights), computes the attack locally, and relies on TRANSFER to the closed-weight production agent. Delivery vectors: posting the obfuscated prompt to prompt marketplaces (PromptBase, ShareGPT) or social media with an enticing false claim, embedding it in a webpage the user asks the agent to summarize, or emailing it. Because the prompt is obfuscated, the victim cannot tell what it does by reading it.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) the attack fundamentally depends on open-weight-to-proprietary TRANSFERABILITY -- for proprietary models with no similar open-weight counterpart the attack may not be possible, and no black-box optimization method is provided (left to future work); (2) it requires white-box gradient access to compute the prompt; (3) transfer can be inconsistent -- prompt T11 on ChatGLM failed completely (0% PII precision/recall, always extracting a single prompt fragment), which the authors attribute to the deployed ChatGLM model likely being much larger/different from the local GLM-4-9B; (4) PII recall is only ~50% (T10 41-47%), so the attack extracts a fraction, not all, of the PII (avg 7.4 PII items per conversation); (5) real-product evaluation used only a SINGLE inference per conversation due to ~35/day quota limits, and 2 conversations were dropped as flagged harmful; (6) word-extraction/PII precision are acknowledged lower-bound estimates (Porter-stemmer collisions; context terms penalize PII precision). Discussed mitigations (restricting the agent's tool set; perplexity-based input filtering) are judged weak -- tool restriction hurts usability, and prior work shows low-perplexity obfuscated prompts are achievable, so perplexity filtering is unreliable.

datasets_used: ShareGPT (Vicuna unfiltered) conversation dataset -- ~53k human/ChatGPT-3.5 conversations; first 100 sampled for training, last 100 held out for validation (information-exfiltration objective)
WildChat (2024) human-LLM conversations -- 49 conversations in the 'Job, visa, and other applications' PII category; 24 used for training, 25 for validation (PII-exfiltration objective)
Alpaca dataset -- source of unrelated questions (15%) mixed into the image-attack question set D_q
GPT-4-generated image-related questions -- 85% of the image-attack question set D_q
quantitative_results: Real product, PII exfiltration, Mistral LeChat prompt T10: 100% syntax correct, 70.4% PII precision, 41.2% PII recall, 88.0% context GPT score
Real product, PII exfiltration, ChatGLM prompt T12: 92% syntax, 34.7% PII precision, 63.0% PII recall, 88.0% context GPT; prompt T11 failed (96% syntax but 0% PII precision/recall)
Real product, info exfiltration, Mistral LeChat T1: 84.7% syntax, 70.1% extraction precision, 66.3% extraction GPT score
Real product, info exfiltration, ChatGLM T5: 99.0% syntax, 62.5% extraction precision, 59.2% extraction GPT score
Local PII exfiltration, Mistral-Nemo T10: 100% syntax, 79.8% PII precision, 47.0% PII recall; T9: 97.2% syntax, 54.3% PII precision, 30.1% recall, 94.8% context GPT

Local info exfiltration text models: syntax correctness >=80% (best prompts near/above 90%), word-extraction precision ~80%; e.g. Mistral-Nemo T1 90.0% syntax / 87.2% precision (PPL 5413), Llama-3.1 T7 79.0% syntax / 90.0% precision
Local visual attack on PaliGemma-3B (6 adversarial images V1-V6): syntax correctness 82.0-87.8%, extraction precision 73.0-86.3%, extraction GPT 61.2-78.4%, SSIM 0.583-0.785
Obfuscation: adversarial-prompt perplexities in the thousands (e.g., 5413, 7489, 39211) computed on Llama-3.1-70B where natural language scores <10
Avg 7.4 ground-truth PII items per validation conversation; ~50% recall means roughly half extracted on average
baselines_compared: Manual (un-obfuscated) natural-language prompts N1-N6 -- the readable equivalents of the optimized prompts; optimized prompts consistently beat them on syntax correctness (e.g., LeChat info-exfil N1 59.1% vs T1 84.7%; LeChat PII N6 44.0% vs T10 100%; ChatGLM PII N6 40% vs T12 92%)
Vanilla GCG (Zou et al.) -- the base algorithm being extended; the paper argues plain GCG cannot converge on this harder context-dependent, precise-syntax objective and does not target obfuscation
Initial (pre-optimization) seed prompts -- reported as the '->' change in perplexity/syntax, showing large improvement after optimization
